g.data-narration: This diagram shows the complete Machine Learning process workflow
Step 1: Define the business problem and convert it into an ML task
g.text: Define business objectives as ML task • Determine success metrics • Check if ML is appropriate
Example: Bank fraud detection → Binary classification (fraud vs legitimate)

g.data-narration: Step 2: Gather data from various sources like databases, APIs, and sensors
g.text: Gather data from databases, APIs, sensors • Ensure privacy compliance (GDPR) • Assess quality

g.data-narration: Step 3: Clean data, perform EDA, engineer features, and transform data
g.text: Cleaning • EDA • Feature Engineering • Feature Selection • Transformation

g.data-narration: Step 4: Choose appropriate ML algorithm based on problem type
g.text: Choose algorithm based on problem type, data size, interpretability needs, computational constraints

g.data-narration: Step 5: Split data and train the model with cross-validation
g.text: Split data • Train on training set • Tune hyperparameters with validation set • Use cross-validation

g.data-narration: Step 6: Evaluate model using appropriate metrics on test data
g.text: Assess on test set (never seen during training) • Compare to baseline • Check for overfitting

g.data-narration: Step 7: Deploy model as API and integrate with production systems
g.text: Package as API • Integrate with production systems • Set up inference infrastructure • A/B testing

g.data-narration: Step 8: Monitor model in production and retrain to handle data drift
g.text: Monitor performance • Detect drift • Retrain with fresh data • Iterate the entire process

g.data-narration: The complete ML pipeline flows from problem to maintenance in an iterative cycle
Key points: problem definition is critical, data prep takes 60-80 percent, and ML is iterative
g.text: Problem definition is most critical • Data prep takes 60-80 percent of time • Train Val Test split essential
Choose metrics by problem type • Monitor for drift in production • ML is iterative
